@vibovitold I’ve watched the entire series, I’m not blind – and I stand by what I said. The show could’ve gone deeper by showing at least one scene with the parents’ reaction of the girl, or giving the girl a voice before her death. That perspective matters. Still, I give it a solid 8–9 because the acting was outstanding.

But let’s be honest: parents and the surrounding environment shape a child’s behavior more than people like to admit. When there’s emotional neglect, lack of communication, or even cruelty at home, it often leads to pain, confusion, and destructive choices. Not every case ends in murder – but many problems could be prevented if people paid more attention.

In most conflicts, it takes two sides. It’s rarely as simple as “victim and villain.” Everyone has a choice, yes – but those choices don’t happen in a vacuum. And when someone ends up dead, that’s not just tragic – it’s the worst possible outcome of many ignored warning signs.

If you don’t want to talk about that part of the story, fine – but don’t pretend it doesn’t exist.

​@Panda-fn7hz 
"The show could’ve gone deeper by showing at least one scene with the parents’ reaction of the girl, or giving the girl a voice before her death. "

It could have done plenty of things (some people here complained there was no courtroom battle in it, they're so used to them in crime dramas).

I think this was a deliberate choice, to keep the story focused. 
Note that we watch how it unfolds mostly from the parents' perspective. 

(The one-shot technique is not a random gimmick - it confines us to "here and now", no jumping places, no retrospections, etc.)

And the parents most likely wouldn't really have had that girl on their radar, let alone before she died.

"But let’s be honest: parents and the surrounding environment shape a child’s behavior more than people like to admit."

Of course. And the parents are explicitly shown tormenting themselves about it.

The point is that the answer isn't always as obvious as "cruelty at home", or sexual abuse etc. 
These are cliches.

So once again it's a deliberate choice, in my personal opinion the right choice.
As Graham himself put it: "I wanted to eliminate the possibility of thinking Eddie is a violent man [...] I wanted Jamie’s mum not to be an alcoholic in any way, shape, or form".

And it corresponds with many real-life cases, where kids who murder someone don't come from troubled families.
There are some hints of how the father and the son weren't on the same wave (despite the dad meaning well), like in these football stories Jamie tells the psychologist, but these are very subtle. 

This story leaves many questions open, it's not supposed to be a parenting / crime prevention manual. 
That elevates it from a trivial cautionary tale. 
But that's a matter of taste.
